coverage: Official access status plus historical guide and recent public discussion; not a live bite report
summary: Historically, anglers describe a busy, exposed pier where wind, surf and crossed lines can shape the session as much as the fishing. Public discussion often leans toward crabbing and mixed-species pier fishing rather than a dependable halibut pattern. The pier is currently closed, so this context is archival until the City announces reopening.

themes.label: Currently closed
themes.note: The City reports the pier closed to the public beginning June 4, 2026.

themes.label: Wind and surf
themes.note: Older reports consistently emphasize exposed conditions and changing water clarity.

themes.label: Crowds and tangles
themes.note: Popular periods can mean limited rail space and crossed lines.

themes.label: Mixed-species history
themes.note: Discussion is broad and should not be interpreted as a current halibut signal.

coverage: Official facility information and historical pier guide; sparse recent discussion and no live catch signal
summary: The recurring picture is a small, shallow-water pier beside mud flats, tidal shelves and the marina edge. Those transitions make the access interesting, but publicly available recent shore reports are thin and do not support claiming a current halibut trend.

themes.label: Shallow and tidal
themes.note: Water level can materially change the fishable edge around the pier.

themes.label: Mud-flat margin
themes.note: Historical descriptions emphasize soft bottom and shelf transitions.

themes.label: Marina edge
themes.note: The dredged basin and marina mouth are the main structural features.

themes.label: Sparse chatter
themes.note: There is not enough recent public discussion for a reliable bite trend.

coverage: Current public-project context plus historical pier material; no reliable recent shoreline trend
summary: Much of the online fishing history refers to Berkeley's closed municipal pier, not the open North Basin shoreline shown in CastingCompass. The useful takeaway is structural—riprap, a marina mouth and a dredged edge—while recent public discussion is too limited to call a present-day shore bite.

themes.label: Shoreline only
themes.note: This location is the public North Basin shore, not the closed municipal pier deck.

themes.label: Riprap edge
themes.note: Rock wall access borders a shallow exterior flat and marina basin.

themes.label: History can mislead
themes.note: Older Berkeley Pier reports should not be treated as reports for this casting zone.

themes.label: No current signal
themes.note: Recent location-specific public discussion is insufficient for a trend.

coverage: Current NPS access notes and historical fishing guide; not a live catch report
summary: Torpedo Wharf is repeatedly described as a high-current, windy urban pier where parking, visitor traffic and crabbing activity affect the experience. Halibut appears in the historical seasonal mix, but the reviewed sources do not establish a fresh bite; uneven pavement is also a current access consideration.

themes.label: Strong current
themes.note: Golden Gate flow and vessel wakes can make presentation and footing more demanding.

themes.label: Wind exposure
themes.note: The open waterfront can be uncomfortable even when inland weather is mild.

themes.label: Limited parking
themes.note: Nearby free and time-restricted spaces can fill quickly.

themes.label: Uneven pavement
themes.note: NPS notes cracked and heaving asphalt on the pier approach and surface.

coverage: Current park information, historical guide and one older public report; not a live bite report
summary: Paradise Beach is generally portrayed as a quieter, family-oriented pier over shallow mud, grass and a narrow rocky-sand edge. Reports describe variable fishing with occasional seasonal halibut, but the public sample is small and older, so it is context rather than evidence of a current bite.

themes.label: Quiet park setting
themes.note: The site is commonly framed as a relaxed shoreline park and pier.

themes.label: Shallow bottom
themes.note: Mud, grass and a narrow edge make tide and current especially relevant.

themes.label: Occasional halibut history
themes.note: Historical mentions are seasonal and too sparse to predict a current bite.

themes.label: Free entry
themes.note: Marin County eliminated park entry fees in 2023; posted hours still apply.

coverage: Official waterfront guidance and historical pier guide; no verified current bite signal
summary: Historical descriptions emphasize deeper urban water, strong current and snag-prone debris, with summer and fall halibut among the seasonal possibilities. Pier 7 also doubles as a busy promenade and photo stop, so sharing space with pedestrians is part of the access experience; none of this is a live catch report.

themes.label: Strong current
themes.note: Tidal flow can be pronounced along the downtown waterfront.

themes.label: Snag potential
themes.note: Pilings and submerged urban debris are recurring historical cautions.

themes.label: Busy promenade
themes.note: Pedestrians and photographers share the narrow pier.

themes.label: Seasonal history
themes.note: Older summer and fall halibut references are not a present-day bite signal.

sources.label: Port of San Francisco fishing guidance

coverage: Current harbor status, historical guide and older forum context; not a live bite report
summary: Discussion distinguishes the calmer, shallower inner harbor from the rocky jetty and dredged entrance edge. Anglers mention a mixed-species fishery, snags and strong tide dependence; the reviewed public posts are too old and broad to support a current halibut claim.

themes.label: Dredged entrance
themes.note: The east side sits near a transition from protected shallows to the harbor channel.

themes.label: Rock and sand
themes.note: Mixed bottom creates structure but can also increase snags.

themes.label: Tide dependent
themes.note: Water movement changes substantially around the harbor entrance.

themes.label: Stable footing only
themes.note: Use dry, stable access and follow posted harbor restrictions.

coverage: Current harbor status, historical guide and older forum context; not a live bite report
summary: The west jetty is described as the more exposed side of the harbor mouth, with a pronounced channel edge, current, rock snags and meaningful wave risk on the outer face. Historical discussion covers a mixed fishery rather than a dependable halibut pattern, and it should not be read as a current bite update.

themes.label: Channel edge
themes.note: The harbor-mouth drop and moving water are the defining structural features.

themes.label: More exposed
themes.note: Outer rocks can be wave-washed even when the inner harbor looks calm.

themes.label: Snag potential
themes.note: Rock structure demands conservative rigging and stable footing.

themes.label: No live trend
themes.note: Reviewed discussion is historical and does not verify present fishing quality.
